children.name: Lemur
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the ring-tailed lemur, arguably the most recognisable of all lemurs. Ring-tailed lemurs are unusual among strepsirrhines in being highly terrestrial and living in female-dominated groups. Their boldly banded tails are held aloft during social encounters.

children.name: Eulemur
children.description: The brown and collared lemurs form the largest genus of Lemuridae, with 13 species distributed across Madagascar's forests from humid east to dry west. Many show striking sexual dichromatism, with males and females bearing conspicuously different coat colours.

children.name: Eulemur albifrons
children.description: A medium-sized brown lemur found in the humid tropical forests of northeastern Madagascar, where males are readily identified by their pale white forehead, cheeks, and beard contrasting with a warm brown body; females are uniformly brown. It lives in groups of 3–12 individuals centred on core ranges within the forest and has a frugivorous diet supplemented with leaves, flowers, and invertebrates. Listed as Vulnerable due to rapid habitat loss from slash-and-burn agriculture and charcoal production.

children.name: Eulemur albocollaris
children.description: A critically endangered lemur restricted to a tiny, severely fragmented range in southeastern Madagascar around the coastal lowlands between the Mananjary and Faraony rivers, inhabiting humid lowland forest remnants. The male is distinguished by a striking pure-white ruff framing a dark face, contrasting with a reddish-brown body. It is one of the world's most threatened primates, with habitat loss from agriculture and charcoal production having reduced its range to a fraction of its historical extent.

children.name: Eulemur cinereiceps
children.description: One of the rarest and most endangered of all lemurs, restricted to a small remnant of humid forest in the far southeast of Madagascar around Tsitongambarika and surrounding areas. Both sexes have grey heads, but males show more contrast with their rufous bodies. Its habitat is under intense pressure from slash-and-burn agriculture and charcoal extraction, and the species is listed as Critically Endangered with an estimated population of only a few thousand individuals.

children.name: Eulemur rufifrons
children.description: One of the most studied of Madagascar's lemurs, the Red-fronted brown lemur inhabits both the humid rainforests of eastern Madagascar and the dry deciduous forests of the west, showing remarkable ecological flexibility. Males bear a distinctive reddish-orange crown and face mask contrasting with a grey-brown body, while females have grey foreheads. It lives in groups of 4–18 individuals with a complex social structure and plays an important role as a seed disperser for large-fruited trees.

children.name: Eulemur rufus
children.description: The Red Lemur is found in the dry deciduous and gallery forests of northwestern Madagascar, where males exhibit a vivid reddish-brown coat and pale face with dark facial markings, while females are grey. It is cathemeral — active both day and night — and shifts its activity pattern seasonally in response to food availability and temperature. Threatened by habitat loss from charcoal production and slash-and-burn agriculture, it is classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Eulemur sanfordi
children.description: Sanford's brown lemur is restricted to the dry deciduous and subhumid forests of extreme northern Madagascar, around Montagne d'Ambre and the Tsaratanana Massif. Males are distinguished by conspicuous white-tipped ear tufts and a pale cheek beard on an otherwise grey face, contrasting with the brown body. It often associates with Crowned Lemurs in mixed-species groups, a behaviour that may reduce individual predation risk. Named after Leonard Cutler Sanford, an American naturalist and philanthropist.

children.name: Varecia
children.description: Ruffed lemurs are the largest of the true lemurs, with a distinctive ruff of long fur around the neck and face. They are unusual primates in building nests for their offspring and in their dispersed fission-fusion social system. Critical seed dispersers for large-fruited trees in Madagascar's rainforests.

children.name: Hapalemur
children.description: Bamboo lemurs are the only primates to specialise almost exclusively on bamboo — including the highly toxic giant bamboo, which they detoxify through physiological adaptations. Five species are endemic to Madagascar and critically threatened by bamboo forest loss.

children.name: Hapalemur alaotrensis
children.description: The Lac Alaotra bamboo lemur is unique among primates as the only one adapted to live in papyrus reed beds and tall grasses rather than forest, inhabiting the marshes surrounding Lac Alaotra — Madagascar's largest lake — in the central-eastern highlands. It feeds on papyrus shoots, sedges, and reeds, and can swim competently across open water between reed beds. Critically endangered with fewer than 2,500 individuals remaining, it is threatened by the drainage of its reed-bed habitat for rice cultivation and by hunting.

children.name: Hapalemur meridionalis
children.description: The Southern bamboo lemur inhabits the humid forests and bamboo thickets of southern and southeastern Madagascar from around Ranomafana south to the Andohahela region. It closely resembles the Grey bamboo lemur but is distinguished by its more southerly range, slightly larger size, and genetic distinctiveness. Like other Hapalemur species it specialises on bamboo shoots and grass stems, and is listed as Endangered due to the ongoing loss of its bamboo forest habitat from clearance and charcoal production.

children.name: Prolemur
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the greater bamboo lemur, the largest of the bamboo specialists. Once thought extinct, rediscovered in 1986. Critically endangered with fewer than 100–200 individuals remaining in fragmented patches of southeastern Madagascar.
